description: Cross-paper edges for the large-values / zero-density DAG. from/to are '<arxiv_id>:<node_id>'; direction is subject-verb-object (from {type} to). Note: 1408.5794 and 2405.20552 are SHARED nodes with the bourgain-demeter-decoupling DAG — that overlap is the cross-DAG link (the decoupling → ζ → large-values descent); see manifest.related_dags.

edges.note: Guth's survey states the Guth–Maynard large-value estimate (its Theorem 6.1) verbatim as Theorem 1.1 of the Guth–Maynard paper.
The 'Guth–Maynard bound' $A(\sigma)\le\tfrac{15}{3+5\sigma}$ catalogued in Tao–Trudgian–Yang is exactly the zero-density estimate of the Guth–Maynard paper.

Tao–Trudgian–Yang's improved Heath-Brown zero-density theorem $A(\sigma)\le\tfrac3{10\sigma-7}$ sharpens the Guth–Maynard bound over part of the range $\tfrac7{10}<\sigma\le1$, by feeding new large-value and additive-energy estimates through the Heath-Brown relation.

Tao–Trudgian–Yang's new exponent pairs improve on Bourgain's $(\tfrac{13}{84},\tfrac{55}{84})$ exponent pair (the '$\zeta$-paper' exponent), which itself came from decoupling-derived exponential sums.

Matomäki–Teräväinen prove zero density ⟹ large value estimates; the Heath-Brown relation (catalogued by Tao–Trudgian–Yang) is the reverse, large value + additive energy ⟹ zero density. Together they make the two essentially equivalent — exactly the kind of structural fact a typed graph surfaces.

Two routes to primes in short intervals: Matomäki–Teräväinen get (conditionally on a power-saving density hypothesis) intervals of length $(\log X)^{2+\epsilon}$ for almost all $x$; Guth–Maynard get (unconditionally) intervals of length $x^{17/30+\epsilon}$.

Starichkova's general theorem 'zero-density exponent $b$ ⟹ primes in $x^{1-1/b+\epsilon}$ intervals' is the abstract mechanism that Guth–Maynard's $x^{17/30+\epsilon}$ result instantiates (with the weighted-zero-density refinements).
The classical zero-density route gives primes in $x^{7/12+\epsilon}$ intervals; Guth–Maynard's improved zero density pushes this to $x^{17/30+\epsilon}$.
Bourgain's large-values theorem and the Montgomery–Halász $MM^*$ method described in Guth's survey are the same family of spectral/duality argument; Tao–Trudgian–Yang's pipeline runs on it.

Montgomery's large value conjecture, stated in both papers (Guth in $\mathrm{LV}_{M,\ell^\infty}$ form, Tao–Trudgian–Yang in $\mathrm{LV}(\sigma,\tau)$ form) — the common target of the whole large-values program.
